impact_assessment: Critical - Attackers bypass authentication controls by targeting legacy endpoints, potentially gaining unauthorized access to user data, sensitive operations, and API resources without valid credentials. Business impact includes data breach, compliance violations (GDPR/HIPAA), and reputational damage.
remediation: 1) Implement unified authentication middleware at gateway/router level before version-specific handlers. 2) Apply identical auth policies across all API versions. 3) Deprecate and sunset old versions with explicit timeline. 4) Add middleware check: `if (!request.headers.authorization && !whitelist_endpoint(path)) { return 401; }` before routing. 5) Monitor legacy endpoint access and alert on non-deprecation-approved traffic. 6) Version control policy documentation ensuring new auth features propagate backward.

recon_method: Passive HTTP header analysis via curl/wget to observe cache behavior and response headers without modifying requests
vulnerability_found: Cache poisoning via unkeyed Host header - CDN fails to include Host header in cache key, allowing attacker to poison cache for legitimate domain by requesting via different Host values
non_weaponized_poc: curl -H 'Host: attacker.com' https://victim-cdn.example.com/index.html -v | grep -i 'cache-key\|x-cache\|via' to demonstrate that responses are cached identically regardless of Host header value, confirming header is not part of cache key
impact_assessment: High - Attacker can serve malicious content (JavaScript injection, credential theft, malware distribution) to legitimate users accessing victim domain. Affects all users hitting the poisoned cache entry until TTL expires or cache is manually purged. Reputation damage and potential compliance violations.
remediation: CDN configuration fix: Ensure Host header is included in cache key via: 1) Enable 'cache key normalization' that includes Host header, 2) Configure cache-control rules to vary on Host header: 'Vary: Host' response header, 3) Implement origin request filtering to reject requests with mismatched Host headers, 4) Use CDN's cache key customization settings to explicitly include Host in the key generation algorithm

recon_method: passive observation of HTTP response headers and cookies during login/logout flow using browser developer tools
vulnerability_found: session token persistence after logout - CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration
non_weaponized_poc: 1) Log in to application and note session cookie (e.g., SESSIONID=abc123). 2) Observe Set-Cookie response headers during logout. 3) Check if session cookie is cleared (Max-Age=0, Expires=past date) or if token remains valid. 4) Document findings in security report without attempting post-logout access.
impact_assessment: High risk: Attackers could reuse captured session tokens after legitimate logout to impersonate users, access sensitive data, perform unauthorized transactions, or maintain persistent backdoor access if session tokens are never invalidated server-side.
remediation: Implement server-side session invalidation on logout: (1) Remove session from backend store (cache/database) on logout request; (2) Set secure cookie flags: Set-Cookie: SESSIONID=; Max-Age=0; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Strict; (3) Implement session token blacklist/revocation list checked on each request; (4) Add server-side timestamp validation; (5) Code example - Node.js/Express: app.post('/logout', (req,res) => { delete req.session; res.clearCookie('SESSIONID'); sessionStore.destroy(sessionId); res.redirect('/'); })

target_system: debug endpoint in production web application
recon_method: passive observation of HTTP error responses and publicly accessible /debug, /health, or /status endpoints without authentication attempts
vulnerability_found: Information Disclosure via Stack Trace Leakage - debug endpoints returning verbose error messages containing internal file paths, framework versions, and system architecture details in production environment
non_weaponized_poc: Send GET request to common debug paths and document returned headers/error messages: curl -i https://target.com/debug/health | grep -E '(path|version|internal|File)' - demonstrates information leakage WITHOUT attempting exploitation or system modification
impact_assessment: Attackers gain reconnaissance data for targeted attacks: internal directory structure enables path traversal targeting, framework version disclosure allows version-specific exploit research, system paths reveal database locations and configuration file paths, reducing attacker effort for subsequent attacks
remediation: 1) Environment-specific error handling: Implement conditional error responses - return verbose stacks only in development/staging, generic messages in production. 2) Disable debug endpoints in production via environment variable checks. 3) Configure application framework error handler: catch exceptions and log full details server-side while returning sanitized client response. 4) Example (Node.js/Express): app.use((err, req, res, next) => { if(process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production') { res.status(500).json({error: 'Internal Server Error'}); } else { res.status(500).json({error: err.message, stack: err.stack}); } }); 5) Implement request filtering to block debug endpoint access in production via WAF/reverse proxy rules.

recon_method: static analysis of mobile application binary using tools like Frida, examining certificate pinning implementation in debug symbols and network security configuration files
vulnerability_found: certificate pinning bypass in debug build - conditional logic that disables certificate validation when BUILD_DEBUG flag is true, located in network layer's SSLPinningDelegate class
non_weaponized_poc: instrumentation script that logs whether certificate pinning is active: uses runtime inspection to read the BUILD_DEBUG flag value and print the state of pinning enforcement without intercepting traffic or modifying certificates
impact_assessment: debug builds distributed accidentally in production could allow man-in-the-middle attacks on API communications, exposing user credentials, session tokens, and sensitive data transmitted between mobile client and backend servers
remediation: remove all debug-conditional certificate pinning logic; implement pinning uniformly across all build configurations; use build-variant specific network security configuration files that enforce pinning in release builds; ensure debug builds never reach production through CI/CD pipeline gating and code review processes that flag any BUILD_DEBUG references in security-critical code paths

target_system: Third-party SaaS integration with OAuth 2.0 authentication
recon_method: Browser developer tools inspection of HTTP headers during OAuth redirect flow; passive observation of network traffic logs
vulnerability_found: OAuth access tokens exposed in HTTP Referer headers when users navigate from OAuth callback pages to external resources
non_weaponized_poc: Document the presence of Authorization header tokens appearing in Referer logs by: 1) Capturing HTTP request headers during OAuth callback, 2) Identifying if tokens persist in referrer when clicking external links, 3) Reviewing server access logs for token patterns in Referer field - no actual token extraction or usage attempted
impact_assessment: High severity - Token leakage enables account takeover, unauthorized API access to user resources, potential lateral movement to connected services, and data exfiltration affecting confidentiality and integrity of user accounts
remediation: 1) Set 'Referrer-Policy: no-referrer' or 'strict-origin-when-cross-origin' HTTP header on OAuth callback pages; 2) Use window.history.replaceState() after token receipt to remove tokens from URL; 3) Store tokens exclusively in httpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookies instead of URL parameters; 4) Implement token rotation policies with short expiration times; 5) Add Content-Security-Policy header to restrict external navigation from sensitive pages

target_system: multi-tenant SaaS application with shared database
recon_method: passive API response analysis - examining HTTP headers, response times, and error messages for tenant identifier patterns without attempting unauthorized access
vulnerability_found: Insufficient row-level security (RLS) in tenant data isolation - tenant_id filtering not applied to all ORM queries in user profile and document retrieval endpoints
non_weaponized_poc: Documentation of the vulnerability discovery process: (1) Authenticate as Tenant A user, (2) Intercept API calls to /api/documents endpoint, (3) Observe that tenant_id parameter is client-controlled in request body rather than server-derived from session, (4) Document that modifying tenant_id value returns data from other tenants without authorization checks, (5) No actual data extraction - only confirmation that isolation mechanism fails
impact_assessment: Critical business impact: unauthorized access to sensitive customer data including PII, financial records, and proprietary documents; potential regulatory violations (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA); loss of customer trust and competitive data exposure; potential legal liability and financial penalties
remediation: Implement server-side tenant isolation: (1) Derive tenant_id exclusively from authenticated session/JWT claims, never from client input; (2) Apply tenant_id filtering at database query level using parameterized queries or ORM middleware; (3) Implement query interceptor pattern to automatically append WHERE tenant_id = ? to all data-access queries; (4) Use database-level RLS policies as defense-in-depth; (5) Add unit and integration tests validating tenant isolation; (6) Implement query logging to detect cross-tenant access attempts; (7) Code example: Change from `User.where(tenant_id: params[:tenant_id])` to `User.where(tenant_id: current_user.tenant_id)` with tenant_id sourced only from verified session data

recon_method: kubectl get svc -A to enumerate exposed services; nmap scanning for common dashboard ports (30000-32767 NodePort range); checking service type with kubectl describe svc kubernetes-dashboard -n kubernetes-dashboard
vulnerability_found: CWE-306 Missing Authentication: Kubernetes Dashboard exposed via NodePort service without network policies or RBAC controls, allowing unauthenticated HTTP access on cluster worker nodes
non_weaponized_poc: curl -v http://<worker-node-ip>:<nodeport>/ to verify dashboard responds without authentication headers; observe HTTP 200 response and dashboard landing page HTML without 401/403 status codes
impact_assessment: Unauthenticated attacker can view cluster topology, running pods/deployments, secrets metadata, service configurations, and cluster resource usage; potential for lateral movement, pod manipulation discovery, or sensitive data exposure from environment variables displayed in pod details
remediation: 1) Change service type from NodePort to ClusterIP: kubectl patch svc kubernetes-dashboard -n kubernetes-dashboard -p '{"spec": {"type": "ClusterIP"}}' 2) Implement NetworkPolicy restricting access to dashboard pods from authorized namespaces only 3) Enable RBAC with minimal ServiceAccount permissions 4) Use kubectl proxy or port-forward for dashboard access: kubectl port-forward -n kubernetes-dashboard svc/kubernetes-dashboard 8443:443 5) Enforce TLS and require bearer token authentication via kubeconfig

recon_method: passive code review of publicly available JavaScript source maps and minified files; static analysis of DOM manipulation patterns
vulnerability_found: Extra Window Memory Injection - insecure window object property assignment allowing unauthorized cross-context data manipulation via window[userInput] = maliciousValue
non_weaponized_poc: Detection script that identifies vulnerable patterns: 1) Search minified/source code for patterns like 'window[' combined with user-controlled variables, 2) Use browser DevTools to inspect window object for unexpected property modifications, 3) Monitor console for warnings about writable window properties that should be read-only
impact_assessment: An attacker could pollute the global window object namespace, potentially: overwrite built-in functions or security-critical variables, cause denial of service through memory exhaustion, interfere with third-party library functionality, bypass security checks that rely on specific window properties, or establish persistence mechanisms across page reloads
remediation: 1) NEVER assign user input directly to window object: avoid 'window[userVar] = value', 2) Use a dedicated namespace object: const APP_STATE = {}; APP_STATE[sanitizedKey] = value, 3) Implement property access controls: use Object.defineProperty() with non-configurable and non-writable flags for security-critical values, 4) Use Object.freeze() on objects that should not be modified, 5) Validate and sanitize all keys before property assignment, 6) Consider using Map/WeakMap for dynamic key-value storage instead of direct object assignment

target_system: web application with background job scheduler
recon_method: HTTP response header analysis and timing side-channel observation of scheduled endpoints
vulnerability_found: Insecure Scheduled Task - Unauthenticated cron job execution via publicly accessible /admin/tasks endpoint revealing job queue status without authentication
non_weaponized_poc: GET request to https://target.com/admin/tasks returns HTTP 200 with JSON showing next scheduled execution times, task names, and execution history without requiring authentication headers
impact_assessment: Business impact includes: (1) Information disclosure of internal task scheduling revealing business logic and data processing workflows, (2) Potential for attackers to infer optimal timing for other attacks when resource-intensive tasks run, (3) Compliance violation if scheduled tasks process sensitive data (PII, financial records), (4) Unauthorized visibility into infrastructure automation
remediation: Implement authentication and authorization checks: (1) Require valid session token or API key for /admin/tasks endpoint, (2) Add role-based access control (RBAC) to restrict task queue visibility to administrators only, (3) Move scheduled task endpoints to protected admin routes with CSRF protection, (4) Implement rate limiting on task status endpoints, (5) Audit logging for all access attempts to scheduler endpoints, (6) Use middleware pattern: `if (!user.hasRole('ADMIN')) { return 403 Forbidden; }` before returning task details

recon_method: passive network traffic analysis using browser developer tools and monitoring WebSocket frames without sending malicious payloads
vulnerability_found: Insufficient WebSocket message validation - missing input sanitization on server-side Socket.IO event handlers
non_weaponized_poc: Documentation of observed WebSocket frame structure showing lack of type checking: monitoring legitimate connection shows server accepts unsanitized string inputs in 'message' event without length limits or content validation
impact_assessment: Potential for stored XSS injection via WebSocket messages, denial of service through oversized payloads, or privilege escalation if socket events control authorization state without proper validation
remediation: Implement server-side socket event validation: (1) Define strict schemas for all socket events using libraries like 'joi' or 'zod', (2) Add input sanitization middleware before event handlers, (3) Enforce message size limits via socket configuration, (4) Validate user permissions before processing privileged socket events, (5) Example fix: 'socket.on("message", (data) => { const schema = Joi.object({content: Joi.string().max(5000).required()}); const {error, value} = schema.validate(data); if(error) return socket.emit("error", "Invalid input"); /* process sanitized value */ });'

recon_method: passive analysis of application logs and audit trail retention policies via public documentation and configuration review
vulnerability_found: Indicator Removal from Tools - Application logs lack tamper-evidence mechanisms and audit trails can be deleted by authenticated users without retention enforcement
non_weaponized_poc: Demonstrate that log entries are stored in a mutable database table without cryptographic hashing or write-once storage; show that application permits DELETE operations on audit log records through administrative functions without immutable versioning
impact_assessment: Attackers with application access can cover tracks after compromise, destroy evidence of malicious activity, breach compliance requirements (HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOC 2), obstruct incident response and forensic investigation, and evade detection systems
remediation: 1) Implement immutable audit logging using append-only storage or write-once media; 2) Generate cryptographic hashes of log entries with chained integrity verification; 3) Enforce strict RBAC preventing log deletion, allowing only authorized SOC personnel with segregated access; 4) Configure syslog/centralized logging to remote syslog server outside application control; 5) Implement log retention policies with legal hold mechanisms; 6) Add digital signatures to critical audit entries; 7) Monitor for bulk log deletions with alerting; 8) Regular integrity verification of logs using cryptographic validation

target_system: web application with file handling capabilities
recon_method: passive analysis of HTTP responses, file upload forms, and error messages; review of publicly available documentation and source code repositories
vulnerability_found: Insecure Archive Extraction - CWE-409: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames; specifically unsafe handling of archive utility operations (zip, tar, rar) allowing path traversal during extraction
non_weaponized_poc: Submit a benign test archive containing a file with path traversal sequence (e.g., '../../../etc/passwd') to the file upload endpoint. Document that the extraction utility processes the malicious path without sanitization by observing: (1) HTTP response codes indicating successful processing, (2) error messages revealing extraction paths, (3) temporary file locations via timing analysis, or (4) monitoring file system access logs during extraction on a test system
impact_assessment: Arbitrary file write to unintended directories, potential code execution if files are written to application directories, privilege escalation, information disclosure, denial of service through disk space exhaustion, compromise of application integrity and confidentiality
remediation: 1) Use archive libraries with built-in path traversal protection (e.g., Python's tarfile with filter='data' in Python 3.12+); 2) Validate all extracted file paths against a whitelist and reject entries containing '..' or absolute paths; 3) Extract to isolated temporary directories with strict permissions; 4) Implement filename sanitization; 5) Set resource limits on extraction operations; 6) Run extraction with minimal required privileges; 7) Scan extracted contents with antimalware tools before processing

target_system: web application with remote management interface
recon_method: passive port scanning and service enumeration (nmap -sV -p 5900-5910 --script vnc-info)
vulnerability_found: Unauthenticated VNC service exposure on port 5900 - VNC server accessible without credentials due to misconfiguration allowing empty password authentication
non_weaponized_poc: Connect to target:5900 using vncviewer or vncprobe to verify VNC service responds to handshake requests and advertise security type None (no authentication required). Document the VNC version string and supported security types without accessing any display data.
impact_assessment: Attacker could gain complete remote desktop access to system running web application, allowing lateral movement, credential harvesting, data exfiltration, malware deployment, and denial of service. Business impact includes service disruption, data breach, and compliance violations (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2).
remediation: 1) Disable VNC on production systems or restrict to localhost only (bind to 127.0.0.1:5900). 2) If remote access required, implement strong VNC authentication: disable 'AllowUnauthenticated=false' in VNC config. 3) Use VNC over SSH tunnel instead of direct exposure. 4) Implement network segmentation - restrict VNC ports via firewall to authorized IPs only. 5) Use VNC security type 'VncAuth' or stronger (TLS) instead of 'None'. 6) Change default VNC password and implement password policy. 7) Disable VNC service entirely if not needed for web application operation.

target_system: Windows-based web application with backend WMI integration
recon_method: Passive analysis of application error messages, HTTP response headers, and public documentation; review of WHOIS/DNS records for infrastructure hints
vulnerability_found: WMI Command Execution via unsanitized user input to WMI queries (CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command)
non_weaponized_poc:
1. Identify WMI query parameter in web form (e.g., 'computername' field)
2. Submit benign test: computername=localhost
3. Observe response indicates WMI execution
4. Log the finding WITHOUT attempting command injection or system modification
5. Document the parameter name and expected behavior only
impact_assessment: Critical - Successful exploitation could allow: remote code execution with application service account privileges, lateral movement within internal network, data exfiltration from database servers, installation of persistent backdoors, denial of service through resource exhaustion via WMI queries
remediation:
1. Use WMI parameter binding/prepared statements: System.Management.ManagementPath with validated scope
2. Implement input whitelist validation before WMI queries (alphanumeric + safe characters only)
3. Run application with least-privilege service account (deny WMI administrative operations)
4. Use Windows Defender for Endpoint to monitor WMI.Sys provider access
5. Example: Replace direct string concatenation with: new ManagementScope(@"\\" + validatedComputerName + @"\root\cimv2")
6. Apply principle of least privilege - restrict WMI namespace access via DCOM permissions

target_system: web application with file upload functionality
recon_method: analyze application's file handling via static code review and directory structure enumeration using publicly available documentation
vulnerability_found: Arbitrary File Upload leading to Remote Code Execution - PHP shell upload vulnerability in /upload endpoint lacking proper file type validation
non_weaponized_poc: Submit multipart form-data with filename 'test.php.jpg' containing PHP code snippet: <?php phpinfo(); ?> - demonstrates the file bypasses extension filtering and can be accessed at /uploads/test.php.jpg, showing that web server executes PHP regardless of double extension
impact_assessment: Critical - Attacker gains ability to execute arbitrary system commands with web server privileges, potentially leading to data breach, lateral movement, malware deployment, and complete system compromise
remediation: Implement defense-in-depth file upload controls: (1) Use whitelist-based MIME type validation via getimagesize() or fileinfo functions instead of extension checking; (2) Store uploads outside web root directory; (3) Rename files to random identifiers stripping original extensions; (4) Set web server to disable script execution in upload directories via .htaccess: 'php_flag engine off' or nginx location blocks; (5) Implement antivirus scanning; (6) Validate file content signatures; (7) Apply strict file permissions (644); (8) Use content-disposition: attachment headers when serving uploads

recon_method: review HTML5 canvas API usage and screenshot library dependencies via source inspection and network analysis
vulnerability_found: Unprotected Screen Capture - HTML5 Canvas/getDisplayMedia API allowing unauthorized screenshot capture without user consent
non_weaponized_poc: Detection script that logs when getDisplayMedia() is available without permission checks: console.log('Screen capture API exposed:', typeof navigator.mediaDevices.getDisplayMedia === 'function'); check for canvas.toDataURL() calls without validation
impact_assessment: Sensitive information disclosure (PII, financial data, credentials visible on screen), compliance violations (HIPAA, PCI-DSS), reputational damage, unauthorized surveillance of user sessions
remediation: 1) Implement Content Security Policy: img-src/media-src restrictions; 2) Check user permissions before getDisplayMedia(): require explicit user consent; 3) Disable right-click/screenshot tools: document.addEventListener('contextmenu', e => e.preventDefault()); 4) Use -webkit-user-select: none CSS; 5) Implement watermarking on sensitive content; 6) Add screenshot detection: monitor visibilitychange events; 7) Require multi-factor authentication for sensitive operations; 8) Log and alert on capture attempts; 9) Use iframe sandboxing with allow-list for trusted content

recon_method: analyze HTTP response headers and JavaScript execution flow using browser DevTools without sending payloads
vulnerability_found: Sensitive data stored in browser memory/JavaScript variables without secure lifecycle management - classified as improper storage vulnerability
non_weaponized_poc: 1) Open browser DevTools Console. 2) Navigate application and observe global window object. 3) Document if authentication tokens, PII, or API keys exist as unencrypted JavaScript variables. 4) Check if data persists after page navigation or survives across tabs. 5) Verify if sessionStorage/localStorage is used instead of memory-only storage.
impact_assessment: If sensitive data remains in memory: attackers could extract via XSS attacks, browser process inspection, or memory dumps. Unauthorized access to user accounts, session hijacking, and data exfiltration possible. Regulatory impact: GDPR/CCPA violations if PII exposed. Customer trust erosion.
remediation: 1) Never store secrets in global JavaScript scope - use private closures or Web Crypto API. 2) Store sensitive tokens in httpOnly, secure cookies only. 3) Implement automatic token expiration and cleanup: 'token = null; delete window.authToken;' after logout. 4) Use Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict inline script execution. 5) Employ variable scoping: wrap sensitive data in IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expressions). 6) Consider sessionStorage with automatic clearing on tab close instead of persistent memory. 7) Implement memory-safe libraries for sensitive operations.

recon_method: passive system information disclosure analysis via HTTP headers and error messages
vulnerability_found: Bootkit vulnerability class: GRUB2 bootloader arbitrary code execution (CVE-2021-3695/3696/3697) - theoretical risk if server allows unauthenticated UEFI firmware modification or unverified boot processes
non_weaponized_poc: reconnaissance script that: (1) queries /proc/cmdline and /sys/firmware/efi to identify bootloader version, (2) checks if Secure Boot is enabled via dmidecode, (3) verifies UEFI partition write permissions, (4) documents findings without attempting modification
impact_assessment: Critical: Bootkit exploitation would allow pre-OS code execution, complete system compromise before web application layer, persistent rootkit installation immune to OS-level remediation, lateral movement to backend infrastructure, data exfiltration at hardware level
remediation: Infrastructure/hardening level: (1) Enable Secure Boot in UEFI settings, (2) implement TPM 2.0 with measured boot attestation, (3) apply signed GRUB2 updates via vendor patches, (4) restrict physical/remote UEFI access with passwords, (5) deploy endpoint detection for firmware anomalies, (6) use immutable infrastructure (read-only boot partitions), (7) regular firmware audits and SBOM tracking of bootloader versions

target_system: web application with user authentication and profile management
recon_method: passive source code review and configuration file inspection via publicly accessible git repositories or cached builds
vulnerability_found: Unvalidated initialization scripts executed during user session startup - specifically startup.sh and login_hook.ps1 in application root directory executing user-controlled environment variables without sanitization
non_weaponized_poc: demonstrates vulnerability existence only: locate initialization script files in deployment package, verify they reference environment variables (e.g., $USER_STARTUP_CMD or %CUSTOM_INIT%), review script content to confirm absence of input validation/sanitization on these variables, document the code path showing script execution during authentication flow
impact_assessment: arbitrary code execution with application service account privileges during user logon, potential for privilege escalation, lateral movement within infrastructure, data exfiltration, malware persistence, and compromise of all user sessions managed by affected application instances
remediation: 1) Remove dynamic script execution from initialization flow; 2) If required, use strict allowlist of permitted startup operations with hardcoded values only; 3) Implement input validation using regex whitelist (alphanumeric + safe symbols only) for any environment variables; 4) Execute initialization scripts with minimal privileges, separate from main application process; 5) Audit initialization script execution with comprehensive logging; 6) Apply principle of least privilege to service account running scripts; 7) Use immutable configuration management (e.g., Terraform, ConfigMap) instead of runtime script modification

recon_method: passive network traffic analysis using tcpdump or Wireshark to observe HTTP vs HTTPS usage patterns without intercepting
vulnerability_found: Mixed Content vulnerability (HTTPS page loading HTTP resources) and missing HSTS header allowing protocol downgrade attacks
non_weaponized_poc: Document all HTTP resource requests observed from HTTPS page using browser developer tools Network tab. Create test HTML page with script tag src='http://example.com/resource.js' served over HTTPS to demonstrate mixed content warning in browser console logs
impact_assessment: Attacker on network path could intercept unencrypted HTTP resources, inject malicious JavaScript, steal session cookies, redirect users to phishing pages, or modify content in transit. Affects confidentiality and integrity of user data. Session hijacking risk for authenticated users
remediation: 1) Update all resource URLs to HTTPS: change

recon_method: passive HTTP header analysis and public metadata extraction
vulnerability_found: Information Disclosure - System Owner/User Discovery via HTTP headers, error messages, and publicly accessible metadata files
non_weaponized_poc: curl -I https://target.com | grep -i 'Server\|X-Powered-By\|X-AspNet-Version'; curl https://target.com/robots.txt; curl https://target.com/.well-known/security.txt; curl https://target.com/admin/ --head to observe 401 responses revealing application framework
impact_assessment: Attackers can identify system architecture, technologies, and potential administrator accounts, reducing reconnaissance time and enabling targeted exploitation. Leaked version information allows for matching against known CVE databases. Username enumeration via error messages or administrative interfaces aids credential attack campaigns.
remediation: 1) Remove identifying HTTP headers: Set 'Server: Apache' (generic) instead of 'Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)'. 2) Configure web server to suppress X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version headers. 3) Implement generic error pages that don't reveal technology stack. 4) Restrict robots.txt to non-sensitive paths only. 5) Disable directory listing and 401 error information leakage. 6) Use rate limiting on admin endpoints. 7) Implement Web Application Firewall rules to normalize response headers. Example nginx config: server_tokens off; proxy_hide_header X-Powered-By; proxy_hide_header X-AspNet-Version;

recon_method: passive DNS enumeration and public cloud metadata analysis via WHOIS, DNS records, SSL certificate transparency logs, and cloud provider documentation
vulnerability_found: Exposed cloud infrastructure metadata and misconfigured DNS records allowing infrastructure reconnaissance
non_weaponized_poc: Script that queries Certificate Transparency logs and DNS records to document infrastructure components: identifies subdomains via crt.sh, checks CNAME records pointing to cloud services (AWS, Azure, GCP), retrieves MX/TXT records, and documents publicly exposed S3 bucket naming patterns without attempting access
impact_assessment: Attackers could map complete infrastructure topology, identify cloud service usage, discover staging/development environments, identify potential attack surface expansion points, and plan targeted attacks against specific infrastructure components
remediation: 1) Implement DNS security: use DNSSEC, minimize TXT/MX record exposure; 2) Remove sensitive hostnames from CT logs by using wildcard certificates; 3) Exclude development/staging domains from public DNS; 4) Implement cloud metadata endpoint protection (IMDSv2 enforcement on AWS); 5) Use consistent naming conventions that don't leak environment types; 6) Monitor CT logs for your domains using services like Censys or crt.sh alerts; 7) Implement network segmentation to limit lateral movement after infrastructure discovery

target_system: Windows web application with server-side code execution capabilities
recon_method: Source code review and static analysis for dangerous function calls; monitoring process creation logs for rundll32 execution patterns
vulnerability_found: Arbitrary code execution via rundll32.exe invocation - CWE-78 (OS Command Injection); located in server-side script processing module that constructs system commands from user input
non_weaponized_poc: Detection script that identifies vulnerable pattern: Grep codebase for patterns like 'rundll32', 'shell_exec', 'system()' or 'exec()' combined with user-controlled variables. Example vulnerable pattern found: $cmd = 'rundll32.exe ' . $_GET['dll_param'];
impact_assessment: Critical - Attacker could execute arbitrary Windows DLL functions with application privileges, leading to complete system compromise, data exfiltration, lateral movement, or deployment of malware. Business impact includes data breach, service disruption, and reputational damage
remediation: 1) Eliminate shell command execution entirely - use language-native APIs instead of rundll32. 2) If shell execution is unavoidable, use allowlist-based input validation and parameterized APIs (proc_open with separate arguments array in PHP). 3) Implement principle of least privilege - run web application under low-privilege service account. 4) Enable Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) or AppLocker to restrict rundll32 execution. 5) Add input validation: reject any user input containing 'rundll32', '.dll', or special shell characters. 6) Implement logging and alerting for any rundll32 process creation from application context.

recon_method: passive analysis of HTTP headers, DNS records, and publicly available documentation without accessing protected resources
vulnerability_found: Container metadata exposure via HTTP headers and environment information disclosure - specifically: X-Powered-By headers revealing container orchestration platform, Docker image layer metadata accessible via registry APIs, and Kubernetes service discovery endpoints documented in public repositories
non_weaponized_poc: curl -I https://target-app.com | grep -i 'server\|x-powered\|x-container' && dig SRV _kubernetes._tcp.default.svc.cluster.local to demonstrate information leakage without exploitation
impact_assessment: Low to Medium severity: Attackers gain reconnaissance data enabling targeted attacks against specific container technologies, facilitates supply chain attack planning, reveals infrastructure topology for lateral movement planning, and reduces attacker time-to-exploit by eliminating platform guessing
remediation: 1) Remove identifying headers: Configure web server to suppress X-Powered-By, Server, and X-Container headers via nginx/apache directives; 2) Restrict registry access: Implement authentication on container registries and disable anonymous layer pulling; 3) Network segmentation: Isolate Kubernetes DNS from external resolution; 4) Use reverse proxy: Implement WAF/reverse proxy to normalize headers before client exposure; 5) Container hardening: Use distroless images and remove unnecessary metadata from container builds

target_system: AWS Lambda-based web application with API Gateway
recon_method: Passive DNS enumeration and HTTP header analysis to identify Lambda execution patterns (X-Amzn-Trace-Id headers, response timing patterns, CloudFront distribution detection)
vulnerability_found: Insecure Lambda environment variable exposure - sensitive credentials stored in plaintext in function configuration accessible via IAM misconfiguration
non_weaponized_poc: Using AWS CLI with proper credentials: 'aws lambda get-function-configuration --function-name <name>' to document that environment variables are readable. PoC only retrieves metadata without attempting to use exposed credentials.
impact_assessment: Exposure of database credentials, API keys, or third-party service tokens could allow unauthorized access to backend resources, data exfiltration, privilege escalation, and lateral movement within AWS infrastructure. Estimated risk: critical if production database credentials exposed.
remediation: 1) Use AWS Secrets Manager or Parameter Store instead of environment variables: Replace 'Environment.Variables' with IAM-authenticated secret retrieval at runtime. 2) Code example: Replace 'const password = process.env.DB_PASSWORD' with 'const secret = await secretsClient.getSecretValue({SecretId: "db-password"})'. 3) Implement least-privilege IAM roles restricting Lambda permissions to only required services. 4) Enable CloudTrail logging for all GetSecretValue calls. 5) Rotate all exposed credentials immediately.

recon_method: passive DOM inspection and JavaScript console analysis during normal user interaction
vulnerability_found: Hidden Window vulnerability - concealed iframe or pop-up window created via JavaScript that is not visible to users but executes code
non_weaponized_poc: document.querySelectorAll('iframe'); inspect for display:none, visibility:hidden, or width/height of 0; check window.open() calls in page source with visibility parameters
impact_assessment: Business impact includes: unauthorized data exfiltration, malware distribution, phishing overlays hidden from user awareness, credential theft through invisible form injection, session hijacking through hidden communication channels, compromise of user trust and potential regulatory violations (GDPR, CCPA)
remediation: 1) Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) with frame-ancestors and script-src directives; 2) Use iframe sandbox attribute with restrictive permissions; 3) Validate and whitelist all iframe sources; 4) Implement Subresource Integrity (SRI) for external scripts; 5) Use security headers: X-Frame-Options: DENY; 6) Regular DOM mutation observation monitoring; 7) Code review process for all window.open() and dynamic iframe creation; 8) Disable eval() and inline scripts; 9) Implement iframe visibility checks in client-side security monitoring

target_system: macOS web application with native code execution
recon_method: Static analysis of compiled binaries using otool and strings utility to identify dynamic library loading patterns without executing code
vulnerability_found: LC_LOAD_DYLIB addition vulnerability - unsigned/unvalidated dynamic library loading in Mach-O binary allows arbitrary dylib injection via DYLD_INSERT_LIBRARIES or direct binary modification
non_weaponized_poc: Demonstration script that: (1) uses 'otool -L' to enumerate existing LC_LOAD_DYLIB entries, (2) uses 'codesign -v' to verify code signature status, (3) documents missing Library Validation entitlement via 'codesign -d --entitlements' - reports findings without modifying any binaries or injecting libraries
impact_assessment: High severity - attacker could achieve arbitrary code execution with same privileges as the application, bypass security controls, steal credentials/data, establish persistence, compromise user system if application runs with elevated privileges
remediation: 1) Add 'com.apple.security.cs.disable-library-validation' set to false in entitlements, 2) Implement code signing with hardened runtime, 3) Use @rpath or @loader_path for library references instead of absolute paths, 4) Validate library code signatures at runtime, 5) Set INSTALL_NAME_TOOL to use relative paths: 'install_name_tool -change /usr/local/lib/libexample.dylib @rpath/libexample.dylib app_binary', 6) Build with -fvisibility=hidden and enable Link Time Optimization

recon_method: examine HTTP response headers and HTML source code for encoding declarations; review cookies and form submissions for character encoding inconsistencies
vulnerability_found: Inconsistent character encoding declaration - HTML meta charset tag differs from HTTP Content-Type header, allowing potential UTF-7/UTF-8 interpretation conflicts that could bypass XSS filters
non_weaponized_poc: Request page and document: HTTP header shows 'Content-Type: text/html; charset=ISO-8859-1' while HTML contains '<meta charset="UTF-8">' - this discrepancy is logged for analysis without attempting payload injection
impact_assessment: Attackers could potentially exploit encoding inconsistencies to bypass input validation and XSS protections, leading to stored/reflected XSS attacks, session hijacking, credential theft, and malware distribution to users
remediation: 1) Set consistent encoding in HTTP header: 'Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8' 2) Ensure HTML meta tag matches: '<meta charset="UTF-8">' 3) Configure web server (Apache/Nginx) to enforce UTF-8: 'AddCharset UTF-8 .html .js .css' in Apache or 'charset utf-8;' in nginx 4) Validate and sanitize all user inputs with encoding-aware functions 5) Use Content-Security-Policy headers to mitigate XSS impact

recon_method: passive source code review and HTML/JavaScript inspection using browser developer tools
vulnerability_found: Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via embedded payloads in user profile fields - specifically the 'bio' field which is rendered without sanitization in user profile pages
non_weaponized_poc: Query the application's public user profile API endpoint to retrieve stored data. Demonstrate that HTML/JavaScript entities in the 'bio' field are rendered as executable code by examining the DOM inspector. Example: checking if '' stored in bio field appears unescaped in page source.
impact_assessment: Attackers could steal session cookies, perform account takeover, redirect users to malicious sites, deface user profiles, or conduct phishing attacks against other users viewing compromised profiles. This affects all authenticated users who view affected profiles.
remediation: 1) Implement output encoding: use framework-native functions (e.g., .textContent instead of .innerHTML in JavaScript, or {{ variable | escape }} in templates). 2) Apply Content Security Policy header: 'Content-Security-Policy: script-src \'self\''. 3) Validate and sanitize on input using a library like DOMPurify or bleach. 4) Store user input as plain text, render with proper escaping. Example fix in Node.js/Express: Use 'express-validator' with escape() or use templating engine auto-escaping.

target_system: iOS web application with local plist storage
recon_method: Analyze application file structure through Xcode project inspection and review Info.plist manifest during static analysis; examine application sandbox permissions in entitlements file
vulnerability_found: Insecure plist file modification - Application.plist stored in Documents directory with world-readable permissions (644), allowing unauthorized modification of application configuration including API endpoints and feature flags
non_weaponized_poc: Security research script that demonstrates the vulnerability exists by: (1) Checking file permissions using 'ls -la' on the plist location, (2) Reading the plist structure with plutil to show it contains sensitive config, (3) Documenting that a non-sandboxed process could modify it - NO actual modification performed
impact_assessment: High severity: Attackers could modify API endpoints to point to malicious servers, disable security features, modify authentication tokens, or inject fraudulent configuration affecting all application users on that device. Could lead to credential harvesting, data exfiltration, or privilege escalation
remediation: Store sensitive configuration in Keychain rather than plist files; if plist required: (1) Move to Library/Preferences with restrictive permissions (600), (2) Implement code signing validation: `SecStaticCodeCreateWithPath()` verification before loading plist, (3) Use NSFileProtectionComplete attribute, (4) Add integrity checks via HMAC-SHA256 of plist contents, (5) Example: `[fileManager setAttributes:@{NSFilePosixPermissions: @(0600)} ofItemAtPath:plistPath error:nil]`

target_system: Linux-based web application server with PAM integration
recon_method: Passive examination of /etc/pam.d/ configuration files and system authentication logs to identify PAM module loading patterns and authentication flow
vulnerability_found: Insecure PAM module loading - world-writable directory in PAM module search path (/lib/security) allowing arbitrary module injection
non_weaponized_poc: Script that verifies directory permissions: stat /lib/security and checks for world-writable bits (mode 777 or 775), demonstrates vulnerability exists without loading malicious modules
impact_assessment: High severity - Attacker could inject malicious PAM modules to intercept credentials, bypass authentication, establish persistence, or escalate privileges affecting all applications relying on system PAM authentication

remediation.configuration_fix: Ensure PAM module directories have restrictive permissions: chmod 755 /lib/security /lib64/security and chown root:root with ownership verification
remediation.code_level_fix: In PAM configuration (/etc/pam.d/), explicitly specify full paths to PAM modules and use 'required' control flag instead of 'optional' for critical modules to prevent fallthrough attacks
remediation.monitoring: Implement file integrity monitoring (AIDE/Tripwire) on /lib/security directory and audit PAM module loading via auditd rules: -w /lib/security/ -p wa

recon_method: examine HTTP response headers, DNS records, and cloud provider metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254 patterns); analyze application error messages for cloud provider signatures
vulnerability_found: Improper Access Control to Cloud Instance Revert Functions - allows unauthorized reversion of application state to previous snapshots/backups through exposed API endpoints or console access
non_weaponized_poc: Identify if GET /api/v1/instance/snapshots returns list without authentication; document that OPTIONS request to /cloud/restore reveals backup restore endpoint; confirm 200 response code (does not execute revert)
impact_assessment: Business impact: Loss of data integrity (application state reverted to older versions), potential data loss of recent transactions/user actions, compliance violations (GDPR, HIPAA if sensitive data in snapshots), operational disruption, potential exposure of secrets if snapshots contain unredacted credentials
remediation: 1) Implement role-based access control (RBAC) requiring multi-factor authentication for revert operations; 2) Add audit logging for all snapshot/restore activities; 3) Disable snapshot revert API endpoints and move functionality behind VPN/bastion host only; 4) Implement time-based retention locks preventing reverts within N days; 5) Encrypt snapshots at rest and in transit; 6) Code-level: validate user permissions before any cloud.revert_instance() call; 7) Require explicit approval workflow with time delays for production reversions

target_system: web application with shell access logging
recon_method: Review application documentation and configuration files for command history settings; examine shell configuration in deployment documentation
vulnerability_found: Improper command history logging configuration - HISTCONTROL environment variable not properly enforced, allowing bash history manipulation through ignorespace or ignoredups settings
non_weaponized_poc: Documentation showing current HISTCONTROL setting via: inspect environment variables in application deployment guide or container configuration files; verify bash_history logging is enabled for audit trails
impact_assessment: Administrative actions and shell commands executed during maintenance or debugging sessions could be hidden from audit logs, reducing forensic visibility during security incidents and compliance audits; potential loss of accountability for privileged operations
remediation: Configure HISTCONTROL=erasedups in application deployment scripts and documentation; implement: HISTFILESIZE=10000 and HISTSIZE=10000 in /etc/bashrc or application startup scripts; enforce read-only history files for audit accounts: chmod 444 ~/.bash_history; use centralized command logging via syslog or auditd instead of relying on shell history; document policy requiring: unset HISTCONTROL or HISTCONTROL='' to ensure all commands are logged

recon_method: passive DNS enumeration and HTTP header analysis via curl/wget without authentication
vulnerability_found: Information Disclosure via HTTP Response Headers - Server version, X-Powered-By, and infrastructure details exposed in public responses
non_weaponized_poc: curl -I https://target.example.com | grep -i 'server\|x-powered-by\|x-aspnet' - demonstrates that sensitive header information is returned in standard HTTP requests without triggering any alarms
impact_assessment: Attackers can identify server software versions to research known CVEs, determine technology stack for targeted attacks, and map infrastructure without authentication. Increases attack surface awareness for threat actors.
remediation: Configure web server to remove or obfuscate identifying headers: (1) Apache: Set 'ServerTokens Prod' and 'ServerSignature Off' in httpd.conf; (2) Nginx: Set 'server_tokens off;' in nginx.conf; (3) Application level: Remove X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version headers via response header filtering middleware; (4) IIS: Disable detailed error messages and use custom error pages; (5) Implement security.txt and implement HTTP security headers (X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy) while minimizing informational disclosures

recon_method: passive SSL/TLS certificate inspection using openssl s_client or public certificate transparency logs (crt.sh)
vulnerability_found: expired or self-signed certificate; weak key exchange algorithms (SSLv3, TLS 1.0); certificate chain validation issues
non_weaponized_poc: openssl s_client -connect target.com:443 -showcerts | grep -i 'notAfter\|notBefore\|issuer'; Review certificate details for: expiration dates, weak algorithms (MD5, SHA1), missing intermediate certificates, or CN/SAN mismatches
impact_assessment: expired certificates trigger browser warnings reducing user trust; weak algorithms enable man-in-the-middle attacks; validation failures allow impersonation; non-compliance with security standards (PCI-DSS, SOC 2); potential service unavailability when certificate expires without renewal
remediation: 1) Implement automated certificate renewal 60+ days before expiration using ACME protocol (Let's Encrypt). 2) Force TLS 1.2+ with strong ciphers (ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384). 3) Use certificates signed by trusted CA with SHA-256 or stronger. 4) Ensure complete certificate chain delivery. 5) Implement OCSP stapling to validate certificate revocation status. 6) Deploy HSTS headers (Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000). 7) Regular automated certificate audits via certificate transparency monitoring.

target_system: Web application with user input forms
recon_method: Static code analysis and DOM inspection - reviewing JavaScript source files and form element structure without executing malicious code
vulnerability_found: Unvalidated third-party JavaScript library with keylogging capability in dependencies; specifically a minified analytics script that captures keystroke events without explicit user consent disclosure
non_weaponized_poc: Code review demonstrating the vulnerability exists: Use browser DevTools to inspect Network tab and identify external script. Review source via npm audit or Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tool to confirm the library contains undocumented keyboard event listeners on all input fields. No actual keystroke capture or transmission occurs during this PoC.
impact_assessment: High risk - sensitive data exposure including passwords, personal information, credit card details, and authentication tokens could be captured and transmitted to unauthorized third parties. Compliance violations (GDPR, CCPA, PCI-DSS). Loss of user trust and potential regulatory fines.
remediation: 1) Remove or replace the vulnerable dependency with a vetted alternative. 2) Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers restricting script sources. 3) Use npm audit and establish Software Composition Analysis in CI/CD pipeline. 4) Add explicit consent mechanism and transparency for any legitimate event tracking. 5) Implement subresource integrity (SRI) hashing for third-party scripts. 6) Regular security audits of dependencies with tools like Snyk or WhiteSource. 7) Sanitize and validate all external script inclusions.

target_system: web application with file upload and download functionality
recon_method: analyze publicly documented API endpoints, review robots.txt, examine HTML form attributes, and check HTTP response headers for file handling mechanisms
vulnerability_found: Path Traversal via Directory Traversal in File Download - user-supplied filename parameter lacks proper validation, allowing traversal sequences
non_weaponized_poc: Request to /api/download?file=../../etc/passwd demonstrates the vulnerability exists by observing if the application attempts to access parent directories; researcher documents the behavior without retrieving sensitive data
impact_assessment: An attacker could read arbitrary files from the server including configuration files, environment variables, private keys, source code, and database credentials, leading to authentication bypass, data breach, and complete system compromise

code_level_fix: Use Path.getCanonicalPath() or Path.normalize() to resolve all traversal sequences before file access
Implement whitelist validation: only allow alphanumeric filenames and specific extensions
Use a mapping system (database/config) rather than user input: Map user_request_id → actual_file_path
Example fix in Java: File file = new File(uploadsDir, filename).getCanonicalFile(); if (!file.getCanonicalPath().startsWith(uploadsDir.getCanonicalPath())) { throw new SecurityException(); }
configuration_fix: Run application with minimal filesystem permissions (principle of least privilege)
Store user files outside webroot in a restricted directory
Use a dedicated file serving library (not custom implementation)
Implement strict Content-Disposition headers to prevent file interpretation

target_system: web application running on Linux/Mac server
recon_method: passive examination of publicly available file permissions through HTTP headers, error messages, and directory listing responses; review of application documentation and GitHub repositories
vulnerability_found: Overly permissive file permissions (644 or world-readable) on sensitive configuration files, private keys, and log directories; world-writable directories (/tmp usage) allowing arbitrary file placement
non_weaponized_poc: curl -I https://target.app to examine headers revealing OS information; request error pages that display file paths; check robots.txt and .git directory exposure; verify configuration file accessibility through path traversal payloads that only read and report permission metadata without modification
impact_assessment: exposure of database credentials, API keys, and private cryptographic material; privilege escalation through world-writable temp directories; information disclosure leading to secondary attacks; compliance violations (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR)
remediation: set sensitive files to 600 (rw-------) or 640 (rw-r-----) for application-readable configs; directories to 750 (rwxr-x---) or 700 (rwx------); store secrets in environment variables or secure vaults rather than files; use umask 0077 in application startup; implement regular permission audits: `find /app -type f -perm /077 -o -type d -perm /077`; disable world-writable directories; implement file integrity monitoring (aide/tripwire)

recon_method: passive observation of login page behavior - examining HTTP response codes, timing differences, and error messages without attempting credentials
vulnerability_found: Insufficient rate limiting on authentication endpoint allowing brute force password guessing attacks
non_weaponized_poc: Documentation of response patterns: HTTP 401 returned consistently within 50ms for invalid credentials, no exponential backoff or account lockout after multiple failures. Test account remains accessible after 100 sequential login attempts over 5 minutes.
impact_assessment: Attackers could enumerate valid usernames and compromise accounts through automated password guessing. High-value targets (admin accounts) could be compromised, leading to unauthorized system access, data breach, privilege escalation, and business continuity disruption.
remediation: Implement multi-layered controls: (1) Rate limiting - enforce maximum 5 login attempts per 15 minutes per IP/username combination with progressive delays; (2) Account lockout - temporarily lock account after 5 failed attempts; (3) CAPTCHA - require verification after 3 failed attempts; (4) Monitoring - log and alert on brute force patterns; (5) Strong password policy - enforce minimum 12 characters with complexity; (6) MFA - implement multi-factor authentication for sensitive accounts; (7) Use time-constant comparison functions to prevent timing attacks on credential validation.

recon_method: passive source code review and HTTP header analysis to identify printing service endpoints
vulnerability_found: Printer access control bypass (PubPrn variant) - unauthenticated access to print queue management endpoints at /api/print/queue and /admin/printer/config
non_weaponized_poc: HTTP GET request to /api/print/queue returns printer configuration details and queue status without authentication tokens, demonstrating information disclosure only
impact_assessment: An attacker could enumerate active printers, retrieve sensitive print jobs containing confidential data, or modify printer configurations to redirect output to unauthorized locations, potentially leading to data exfiltration or denial of service
remediation: Implement authentication middleware on all print-related endpoints; validate user authorization before accessing /api/print/* resources; add role-based access control (RBAC) to printer management functions; require valid session tokens for print queue queries; implement rate limiting on printer endpoints; sanitize and validate all printer configuration inputs; log all printer access attempts

target_system: E-commerce web application with product catalog
recon_method: Passive analysis of HTTP response headers, public API documentation review, and inspection of client-side JavaScript source maps
vulnerability_found: Information Disclosure - Sensitive Technical Data Exposure in API Responses (CWE-200)
non_weaponized_poc: GET /api/products/{id} returns JSON response containing internal database schema version, server technology stack (framework/version), database connection pool size, and internal IP addresses in error messages. Researcher documents the presence of these fields without extracting or misusing data.
impact_assessment: Attackers can map internal infrastructure details, identify known vulnerabilities in specific software versions, plan targeted attacks, and bypass security assumptions based on hidden technology stack information. Compliance impact: potential violation of data minimization principles in GDPR/PCI-DSS.
remediation: 1) Implement response sanitization filter to exclude sensitive technical metadata from all API responses. 2) Add API gateway response transformer to strip headers like 'Server', 'X-Powered-By', 'X-AspNet-Version'. 3) Configure custom error responses that omit stack traces and internal details. 4) Implement field-level serialization controls in API serializers to exclude internal technical properties from JSON output. 5) Add security headers: X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options: DENY. 6) Conduct regular API response audits using automated scanning tools.

target_system: web application with backend process execution
recon_method: passive observation of HTTP response headers and error messages; analysis of publicly disclosed dependency vulnerabilities
vulnerability_found: OS Credential Dumping via insecure logging - sensitive credentials (API keys, database passwords) written to application logs stored in world-readable /var/log directory
non_weaponized_poc: 1) Review application source code for hardcoded credentials in debug logging statements. 2) Check file permissions: `ls -la /var/log/app.log` to verify readability. 3) Grep log contents for patterns like 'password=', 'api_key=', 'token=' to demonstrate credential presence without exfiltration.
impact_assessment: Unauthorized database access, lateral movement to connected systems, service impersonation, data breach of customer records, regulatory compliance violations (GDPR, PCI-DSS, HIPAA depending on data types)
remediation: 1) Implement structured logging that excludes sensitive values: use redaction filters before writing. 2) Set restrictive file permissions (chmod 640) on log files. 3) Use environment variables or secure vaults (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) for credentials, never log them. 4) Example code: Replace `logger.info(f'Connecting as user {username} password {password}')` with `logger.info(f'Connecting as user {username}')`. 5) Implement log aggregation with built-in credential masking tools (ELK Stack, Splunk configured with regex filters).

recon_method: Passive source code analysis and dependency mapping via public repository inspection (GitHub, npm registry, PyPI) without accessing production systems
vulnerability_found: Shared Module Dependency Vulnerability - Multiple applications depend on a common internal npm package (@company/auth-utils v1.2.0) that contains hardcoded credentials and insecure session handling logic
non_weaponized_poc: Document the shared module's presence by: 1) Reviewing package.json files in public repos showing dependency on @company/auth-utils, 2) Analyzing the module's source code in the registry to identify the hardcoded connection string in config/database.js line 24, 3) Creating a simple test that instantiates the module and logs its configuration object structure (without executing against live systems)
impact_assessment: Compromise of the shared module affects all 12+ dependent applications across the organization. Exposure of hardcoded database credentials allows unauthorized data access to customer databases. Insecure session handling permits session hijacking and privilege escalation across multiple services. Estimated impact: full compromise of authentication/authorization layer, potential breach of PII data for thousands of users
remediation: 1) Remove hardcoded credentials from @company/auth-utils and implement environment-based configuration using process.env or AWS Secrets Manager, 2) Refactor session handling to use secure, stateless JWT tokens with proper signing and expiration, 3) Implement dependency pinning and automated security scanning in CI/CD pipeline, 4) Create a shared security library review process before publishing internal packages, 5) Bump version to v2.0.0 with breaking changes and enforce update across all dependent applications, 6) Add OWASP security checks to package publication workflow

target_system: web application with external configuration repository
recon_method: passive analysis of public git repositories, GitHub reconnaissance, examining .git/config files accessible via web root, reviewing publicly available documentation and configuration management system artifacts
vulnerability_found: Sensitive credentials and API keys exposed in configuration repository metadata - specifically database connection strings, API tokens, and OAuth secrets committed to version control history accessible at /.git/config or through GitHub API enumeration
non_weaponized_poc: curl -s https://target.com/.git/config | grep -i 'url\|password' && git log --all --oneline | head -20 && git show <commit>:config.yml | grep -E 'api_key|password|secret' (demonstrates exposure without accessing or modifying data)
impact_assessment: Critical - Exposed credentials enable unauthorized database access, API impersonation, lateral movement to backend systems, potential data exfiltration affecting all user records, regulatory compliance violations (GDPR, PCI-DSS), reputational damage, and financial liability from security breaches
remediation: 1) Remove sensitive data from git history: git filter-branch --tree-filter 'rm -f config.yml' 2) Implement pre-commit hooks to block credential patterns: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks 3) Use environment variables and secure secret management (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) 4) Add .gitignore entries for config files: echo 'config/secrets.yml' >> .gitignore 5) Enable repository secret scanning (GitHub Advanced Security) 6) Rotate all exposed credentials immediately 7) Ensure .git directory is not web-accessible via server configuration

target_system: web application with file management functionality
recon_method: analyze application file handling endpoints through documentation review and HTTP request logging; examine error messages and response patterns for file operation clues
vulnerability_found: Insufficient input validation on file deletion endpoint allowing directory traversal with recursive deletion capability
non_weaponized_poc: Send authenticated request to DELETE /api/files with payload {"path": "../../../"} and observe API response indicating traversal capability; document that system accepts relative paths without normalization; verify through file listing endpoint that directories remain intact (do NOT execute deletion)
impact_assessment: Critical business impact: unauthorized deletion of application data, user files, system configuration files, and database backups could result in complete data loss, service unavailability, regulatory compliance violations (GDPR/HIPAA), and financial damages from business interruption
remediation: 1) Implement strict path canonicalization: use Java's Files.getCanonicalPath() or equivalent to resolve all symlinks and relative paths, then verify resolved path is within allowed directory; 2) Use allowlist-based file access with whitelist of permitted directories; 3) Implement proper authorization checks verifying user owns target file/directory before deletion; 4) Add rate limiting on file operations; 5) Require explicit confirmation for recursive deletes; 6) Implement immutable audit logs of all file operations; 7) Use parameterized APIs rather than string concatenation for file paths

target_system: web application with public API endpoints
recon_method: passive analysis of HTTP response headers, rate-limit headers (X-RateLimit-*), and documented API specifications to identify flood vulnerability signatures
vulnerability_found: Insufficient rate limiting on API endpoints - direct network flood vulnerability allowing unauthenticated requests without adaptive throttling mechanisms
non_weaponized_poc: curl -v https://target.com/api/endpoint -H 'User-Agent: SecurityAudit/1.0' | grep -i 'rate-limit' to verify absence of rate-limit headers and response time degradation patterns across sequential requests (documentation only, no actual flood traffic)
impact_assessment: Denial of Service (DoS) - legitimate users unable to access services during attack window; potential infrastructure cost overages from DDoS mitigation services; reputational damage; SLA violations; downstream service failures if APIs are internal
remediation: Implement rate limiting: (1) Deploy reverse proxy/WAF with token bucket algorithm (e.g., nginx limit_req module with configurable requests per second); (2) Add adaptive rate limiting based on client IP/API key with exponential backoff; (3) Implement CAPTCHA challenges after threshold; (4) Use CloudFlare/AWS Shield for upstream DDoS protection; (5) Monitor and alert on request spike detection; (6) Code example - Express.js: app.use(require('express-rate-limit')({windowMs: 15*60*1000, max: 100})) for 100 requests per 15 minutes per IP

target_system: web application with user profile management
recon_method: passive source code review and API documentation analysis without authentication
vulnerability_found: Stored Data Manipulation via insufficient input validation in user profile update endpoint (/api/v1/user/profile)
non_weaponized_poc: Demonstration that the application accepts and stores unvalidated JSON data in user profile fields: sending {"role": "admin", "verified": true} in profile update requests shows these fields are persisted without sanitization. This proves the vulnerability exists without executing harmful actions.
impact_assessment: Unauthorized privilege escalation allowing attackers to self-assign administrative roles, modify account verification status, alter payment information, or manipulate data visible to other users. Could lead to unauthorized access, fraud, data integrity compromise, and regulatory violations (GDPR, PCI-DSS).
remediation: Implement server-side input validation and allowlist approach: (1) Define strict schema for profile updates excluding sensitive fields like 'role' and 'verified'. (2) Use schema validation library (e.g., Joi, Yup): const schema = Joi.object({username: Joi.string().alphanum().max(30), bio: Joi.string().max(500), avatar_url: Joi.string().uri()}); (3) Apply validation before database operations: const {error, value} = schema.validate(req.body); if(error) return res.status(400).json({error: error.details}); (4) Use parameterized queries/ORM to prevent injection. (5) Implement audit logging for data modifications. (6) Add field-level access controls in application logic.
